007
008                                                A BSTRACT
009            Text-to-image models are trained using large datasets collected by scraping image-
010            text pairs from the internet. These datasets often include private, copyrighted,
011            and licensed material. Training models on such datasets enables them to generate
012            images with such content, which might violate copyright laws and individual
013            privacy. This phenomenon is termed imitation – generation of images with content
014            that has recognizable similarity to its training images. In this work we study the
015
               relationship between a concept’s frequency in the training dataset and the ability of
               a model to imitate it. We seek to determine the point at which a model was trained
016
               on enough instances to imitate a concept – the imitation threshold. We posit this
017
               question as a new problem: Finding the Imitation Threshold (FIT) and propose
018            an efficient approach that estimates the imitation threshold without incurring the
019            colossal cost of training multiple models from scratch. We experiment with two
020            domains – human faces and art styles – for which we create four datasets, and
021            evaluate three text-to-image models which were trained on two pretraining datasets.
022            Our results estimate the imitation threshold of these models is in the range of
023            200-600 images, depending on the domain and the model. The imitation threshold
024            can provide an empirical basis for copyright violation claims and acts as a guiding
025            principle for text-to-image model developers that aim to comply with copyright
026
               and privacy laws. Code will be released upon publication.
027
028   1    I NTRODUCTION
029
030   The progress of multi-modal vision-language models has been phenomenal in recent years (Ramesh
031   et al., 2021; Rombach et al., 2022; Goodfellow et al., 2022), much of which can be attributed to the
032   availability of large-scale pretraining datasets like LAION (Schuhmann et al., 2022). These datasets
033   consist of semi-curated image-text pairs scraped from Common Crawl, which leads to the inclusion
034   of explicit, copyrighted, and licensed material (Cavna, 2023; Hunter, 2023; Vincent, 2023; Jiang
035
      et al., 2023; Birhane et al., 2021). Training models on such images may be problematic because
      text-to-image models can imitate — generate images with highly recognizable features — concepts
036
      from their training data (Somepalli et al., 2023a; Carlini et al., 2023a). This behavior has both legal
037
      and ethical implications, such as copyright infringements as well as privacy violations of individuals
038   whose images are present in the training data without consent. In fact, a large group of artists sued
039   Stability AI, developers of the widely-used text-to-image models, alleging that the company’s models
040   generated images that distinctly replicated their artistic styles (Saveri & Butterick, 2023).
041   Previous work has focused on detecting when models memorize training images (Somepalli et al.,
042   2023a; Carlini et al., 2023a), and mitigation techniques (Somepalli et al., 2023b; Shan et al., 2023).
043   For instance, researchers found that duplicate images increase the chance of memorization. Typically,
044   memorization refers to the replication of a specific training image. Instead of measuring memorization,
045   we focus on imitation - a broader and under-explored sense of memorization, where a concept is
046   recognizable from a generated image.
047
      In this work, we ask how many instances of a concept does a text-to-image model need to be
048   trained on to imitate it, where concept refers to a specific person or a specific art style, for example.
049   Establishing such an imitation threshold is useful for several reasons. First, it offers an empirical basis
050   for copyright infringement and privacy violation claims, suggesting that if a concept’s prevalence is
051   below this threshold, such claims are less likely to be true (Saveri & Butterick, 2023; Vincent, 2023;
052   Ceoln, 2022). Second, it acts as a guiding principle for text-to-image models developers that want to
053   avoid such violations. Finally, it reveals an interesting connection between training data statistics and
      model behavior, and the ability of models to efficiently harness training data (Udandarao et al., 2024;

067   Figure 1: An overview of FIT, where we seek the imitation threshold – the point at which a model was exposed
068   to enough instances of a concept that it can reliably imitate it. The figure shows four concepts (e.g., Van Gogh’s
069   art style) that have different counts in the training data (e.g., 213K for Van Gogh). As the image count of a
070   concept increases, the ability of the text-to-image model to imitate it increases (e.g. Piet Mondrian and Van
      Gogh). We propose an efficient approach, MIMETIC2 , that estimates the imitation threshold without training
071
      models from scratch.
072
073   Carlini et al., 2023b). We name this problem FIT: Finding the Imitation Threshold, and provide a
074   schematic overview of this problem in Figure 1.
075   The optimal methodology to measure the imitation threshold requires training multiple models with
076   varying number of images of a concept and measuring the ability of the these models to imitate it.
077   However, training even one of these models is extremely expensive (Bastian, 2022). We propose
078   a tractable alternative, Measuring Imitation ThrEshold Through Instance Count and Comparison
079   (MIMETIC2 ), that estimates the threshold without incurring the cost of training multiple models.
080   We start by collecting a large set of concepts (e.g., various kinds of art styles) per domain (e.g.,
081
      domain of art styles), and use a text-to-image model to generate images for each concept. Then, we
      compute the imitation score of each concept by comparing the generated images of that concept to its
082
      training images, and also estimate each concept’s frequency in the training data. Finally, by sorting
083
      the concepts based on increasing frequency, we estimate the imitation threshold for that domain using
084   a change detection algorithm Killick et al. (2012).
085
      As we operate with observational data, a naive approach may be confounded by several factors, such
086   as the quality of the imitation scoring model on different groups within the domain, or estimating the
087   training frequencies of concepts (e.g., simple counts of ‘Van Gogh’ in the captions results in a biased
088   estimate since the artist may be mentioned in the caption without their painting in the corresponding
089   image). We carefully tailor MIMETIC2 to minimize the impact of such confounders (§5).
090   Overall, we formalize a new problem – Finding Imitation Threshold (FIT; §3), and propose a method,
091   MIMETIC2 , that efficiently estimates the imitation threshold for text-to-image models (§5). We use
092   our method to estimate the imitation threshold for two domains on four datasets, three text-to-image
093   models that were trained on two pretraining datasets (§4). We find the imitation thresholds to range
094   between 200 to 600 images, including tight error bounds for each setting, providing concrete insights
095   on models’ imitation abilities (§6).
096
097   2    BACKGROUND
098   Text-to-Image Models Generating images from textual inputs is a long studied problem, with
099   diffusion models as the current state-of-the-art (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021).
100   Diffusion models are generative models that learn to approximate the underlying data distribution
101   (conditioned or unconditioned). Given a trained diffusion model, it is possible to sample synthetic
102   images from the learned distribution by performing a sequence of denoising operations. Open-sourced
103   models like Stable Diffusion (SD) Rombach et al. (2022) are trained using large datasets such as
104
      LAION (Schuhmann et al., 2022), a large image-caption dataset scraped from Common Crawl.
105   Dataset Issues and Privacy Violations The advancement in text-to-image capabilities, largely due
106   to big training datasets, is accompanied by concerns about the training on explicit, copyrighted, and
107   licensed material Birhane et al. (2021) and imitating such content when generating images (Cavna,
      2023; Hunter, 2023; Vincent, 2023; Jiang et al., 2023). For example, Birhane et al. (2021) and Thiel


                                                                      2
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


108
      (2023) found several explicit images in the LAION dataset and Getty Images found that LAION
109   had millions of their copyrighted images Vincent (2023). Issues around imitation of training images
110   has especially plagued artists, whose livelihood is threatened Saveri & Butterick (2023); Shan et al.
111   (2023), as well as individuals whose face has been used without consent to create inappropriate
112   content Hunter (2023); Badshah (2024). The imitation threshold would be a useful basis for copyright
113   infringement and privacy violation claims in such cases.
114
      Training Data Statistics and Model Behavior Pretraining datasets are a core factor for explaining
115   model behavior (Elazar et al., 2024). Razeghi et al. (2022) found that the few-shot performance of
116   language models is highly correlated with the frequency of instances in pretraining dataset. Udandarao
117   et al. (2024) bolster this finding by demonstrating that the performance of multimodal models on
118   downstream tasks is strongly correlated with a concept’s frequency in the pretraining datasets. In
119   addition, Carlini et al. (2023b) show that language models more easily memorize highly duplicated
120   sequences. We find a similar phenomenon: increasing the number of images of a concept increases
121   the similarity between its generated and training images.
122
123   3       P ROBLEM F ORMULATION AND OVERVIEW
124
      Finding the Imitation Threshold (FIT) seeks to find the minimal number of images with a concept
125   (e.g., a specific art style) a text-to-image model has to see during training in order to imitate it.
126
      FIT’s setup involves a training dataset D ≜ {(xi , yi ) | xi ∈ X , yi ∈ Y}ni=1 , composed of n
127   (image, caption) pairs, where X and Y represents space of images and text captions, respectively and
128   a model M that is trained on D to generate an image x given the text-caption y. From hereafter,
129   M(y) represents the generated image for a provided caption y. Let Ij (x) be an indicator function
                                                                                                    P that
130   indicates whether a concept Z j is present in an image x. Each concept Z j appears cj = i Ij (xi )
131   times in the dataset D, where the dataset may contain multiple concepts {Z 1 , Z 2 , . . .}. Lastly, Mjk
132   represents a model that is trained on a dataset where cj = k.
133   The imitation threshold is the minimal number of training images containing some concept Z j from
134   which the model M generates images M(pj ) (for different random seeds) where Z j is recognizable
135   as the concept, where prompt pj mentions the concept Z j .1 For example, if Z j refers to Van Gogh’s
136   art style, then the imitation threshold is the minimal number of training images of Van Gogh’s
137   paintings in a dataset which, when used to train a text-to-image model, can generate paintings that
138   have Van Gogh’s art style (i.e., imitate Van Gogh’s art style).
139                     \begin {aligned} \textit {Imitation Threshold}^{\;j} & \triangleq \min \left \{ k \in \{1, \dots , n \} : \mathbb {I}^j(\mathcal {M}^j_k(\mathbf {p}^j)) = 1 \right \} \end {aligned}    (1)
140
141   Optimal Approach Finding the imitation threshold is a causal question; the optimal manner of
142
      answering this question is the counterfactual experiment Pearl (2009). For each concept Z j that
143   we want to measure the imitation threshold for, we create training datasets {D1j , D2j , . . . , Dkj , . . . }
144   where k denotes the number of images containing Z j that are added to a large training dataset D (that
145   initially has no images containing Z j ), and then train a model Mjk on each dataset Dkj . Once we find
146   a model, Mjk which is able to generate images that recognizably contains the concept Z j , but Mjk−1
147   cannot, we deem k as the imitation threshold for that concept. However, due to the extreme costs of
148   training even one text-to-image model (Bastian, 2022), this approach is impractical 2 .
149   MIMETIC2 Instead, we propose an approach that is tractable and estimates the imitation threshold
150   under certain assumptions (discussed later). The key idea is to use observational data instead of
151   training multiple models with a different number of images of a concept. Such an approach has been
152   previously used to answer causal questions, inter alia, Pearl (2009); Lesci et al. (2024); Lyu et al.
153   (2024). Concretely, we collect several concepts from the same domain (e.g., art styles) while ensuring
154   that these concepts have varying image counts in the training dataset D of a pretrained model M.
155
      Then, we identify the count where the model M starts imitating concepts, and deem this count to be
      the imitation threshold for that domain. Note that, this threshold is domain specific and not concept
156
      specific, where a domain is defined as a set that contains several concepts belonging to the same
157
      abstract set as the specific concept for which we want to measure the imitation threshold. E.g., if
158   concept refers to Van Gogh’s art style, then a domain would refer to a set of art styles.
159
          1
160       Prompts p are usually different from the training data captions, y.
161       Naively, it will require training O(log m) models for concept Z j (where m is the total number of available
          2

      images of concept Z j ), with an estimated cost of $10M for m = 100, 000 (see Appendix N).


                                                                                                                   3
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


                     Samara Joy            Jacquetta Wheeler            James Garner            Stephen King              Johnny Depp
162
163
164
165
166
167
168   Figure 2: Examples of real celebrity images (top) and generated images from a text-to-image model (bottom)
169   with increasing image counts from left to right (3, 273, 3K, 10K, and 90K, respectively). The prompt is “a
      photorealistic close-up image of {name}”.
170
171   To evaluate the imitation ability of a model, we build two functions, concept-count and imitation-score
172   that counts the number of images of a concept and measures the imitation of a concept in the generated
173   images, respectively. For a domain like art styles, we collect a set of concepts {Z 1 , Z 2 , . . .} (e.g., a
174   list of various art styles), estimate each concept’s count in training data D, and measure the imitation
175   score for each concept using their generated and training images. Sorting the concepts based on their
176   increasing image counts, and using a standard change detection algorithm on the imitation scores,
177
      gives us the imitation threshold for that domain. We provide the implementation details in Section 5.
178   Assumptions To estimate the imitation threshold using observational data, we make three assumptions.
179   First, we assume distributional invariance between the images of all concepts in a domain. Under this
180   assumption, measuring the imitation score of a concept Z j for a counterfactual model Mk′ that is
181   trained with k ′ images of Z j is equivalent to measuring the imitation score of another concept Z i
182   that currently has k ′ images in the already trained model M. This assumption helps us estimate the
183
      causal effect of training a model with a different number of images of a concept, without retraining
      models (we empirically test the validity of this assumption, which we find it to hold) and estimate the
184
      imitation thresholds considering situations when this assumption is loosened(§6). Second, we assume
185
      that there are no confounders between the imitation score and the image count of a concept. These
186
      assumptions are a standard practice when answering causal questions using observational data Pearl
187   (2009); Lesci et al. (2024); Lyu et al. (2024). Third, we assume each image of a concept contributes
188   equally to the learning of the concept. This assumption is commonplace in sample complexity works
189   (Valiant, 1984; Udandarao et al., 2024; Wen et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2017). These assumptions
190   might not hold true in general, however, they are crucial for estimating the imitation threshold in a
191   tractable manner and have been used in prior works. We also tailor our experimental setup such that
192   these assumptions are adhered to as much as possible (§4).
193

201
      Text-to-image Models and Training Data We use Stable Diffusion (SD) as the text-to-image models
202
      Rombach et al. (2022). We use them because both the models and their training datasets are open-
203   sourced. Specifically, we use SD1.1 and SD1.5 that were trained on LAION2B-en, a 2.3 billion
204   image-caption pairs dataset, filtered to contain only English captions. In addition, we use SD2.1 that
205   was trained on LAION-5B, a 5.85 billion image-text pairs dataset, which includes LAION2B-en, and
206   other image-caption pairs from other languages Schuhmann et al. (2022).
207
      Domains and Concepts We experiment with two domains – art styles             and human faces      that
208   are highly important for privacy and copyright considerations of text-to-image models. Figures 1 and
209   2 show examples of real and generated images of art styles and human faces. We collect two sets
210   of concepts for each domain. For art styles we collect classical and modern art styles ,where each
211   artist’s painting are considered a distinct concept, and for human faces, we collect celebrities and
212   politicians. These sets are independent (i.e., have no common concept) and are therefore useful to test
213   the robustness of the thresholds (§6). Each set has 400 concepts that cover a wide frequency range in
214   the pretraining datasets. Appendix M provides details of the sources used to collect the concepts and
215   the sampling procedure. Table 1 summarizes the domains, sets used for each domain, the models and
      their pretraining datasets we experiment with.

      Figure 3: Overview of MIMETIC2 ’s methodology to estimate the imitation threshold. In Step 1, we estimate
227
      the frequency of each concept (belonging to a domain) in the pretraining data by obtaining the images that
228   contain the concept of interest. In Step 2, we use the filtered images of each concept (obtained in Step 1) and
229   compare them to the generated images to measure imitation (using g that receives training and generated images).
230   We repeat this process for each concept to generate the imitation score graph, and then determine the imitation
231   threshold with a change detection algorithm.
232
      Image Generation We generate images for each domain by prompting models with five prompts
233
      (Table 2). We design domain-specific prompts that encourage the concepts to occupy most of the
234
      generated image, which simplifies the imitation score measurement. We also ensure that these
235   prompts are distinct from the captions used in the pretraining dataset to minimize reproduction
236   of training images. (Somepalli et al. (2023b) proposed this strategy to mitigate reproduction of
237   training images.) Out of the five prompts used to generate images, only one of them was found in the
238   LAION2B-en dataset caption 3 times out of the total 2.3B captions. We generate 200 images per
239   concept using different random seeds for each prompt, a total of 1,000 images per concept.
240
241   5     P ROPOSED M ETHODOLOGY: MIMETIC2
242   We illustrate our proposed methodology in Figure 3. At a high level, for a specific domain,
243   MIMETIC2 estimates the frequency of each concept in the pretraining data (Section 5.1) and
244   estimates the model’s ability to imitate it (Section 5.2). We then sort the concepts based on their
245   frequencies, and find the imitation threshold using a change detection algorithm (Section 5.3).
246
      5.1   C ONCEPT F REQUENCY
247
248   Challenges Determining a concept’s frequency in a multimodal dataset can be achieved by employing
249   a high-quality classifier for that concept over every image and counting the number of detected images.
250   However, given the scale of modern datasets with billions of images, this approach is expensive and
251   time consuming. Instead, we make a simplifying assumption that a concept is present only if the
252
      image’s caption mentions it. We further discuss this assumption and provide supporting evidence for
      its accuracy in Appendix D. In addition, concepts often do not appear in the corresponding images,
253
      even when they are mentioned in the captions. For instance, Figure 4 showcases images whose
254
      captions contain “Mary Lee Pfeiffer”, but such images do not always include her. On average, we
255   find that concepts occur only in 60% of the images whose caption mentions the concept.
256
257   Estimating Concept Frequency We start by retrieving
258
      all images whose caption mentions the concept of interest
      and then further filter out the images that do not contain
259
      the concept, as detected by a classifier. We retrieve these Figure 4: LAION images whose caption men-
260
      images using WIMBD (Elazar et al., 2024), a search tool tions ‘Mary Lee Pfeiffer’, the mother of Tom
261   based on a reverse index that efficiently finds documents Cruise. She is not always present in the im-
262   (captions) from LAION containing the search query (con- ages (e.g., the rightmost image).
263   cept). In addition, for each concept, we construct a set of high quality reference images. For example,
264   a set of images with only the face of a single person (e.g., Brad Pitt). We collect these images auto-
265   matically using a search engine, followed by a manual verification to vet the images (see Appendix E
266   for details). These images are used as gold reference for automatic detection of these concepts in the
267   images from the pretraining datasets (we collect up to ten reference images per concept).
268   To classify whether a candidate image from the pretraining data contains the concept of interest, we
269   embed the candidate image and the concept’s reference images using an image encoder and measure
      the similarity between the embeddings. We use a face embedding model (Deng et al., 2022) for faces


                                                                                5
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


270
      and an art style embedding model (Somepalli et al., 2024) for art style. If the similarity between a
271   candidate image and any of the reference images is above some threshold, we consider that candidate
272   image to contain the concept. This threshold is established by measuring the similarity between
273   images of the same concepts and images of different concepts which maximizes the true positives,
274   and minimizes false positives. We provide additional details on how we find the exact thresholds for
275   both art styles and human faces in Appendices F and G.
276   Finally, we employ the classifier on all candidate images corresponding to a concept (i.e., images
277   whose caption mentions the concept), and take those that are classified as positive. For each concept,
278   we randomly retrieve up to 100K candidate images. We use the ratio of positive predictions to the
279   total number of retrieved candidate images, and multiply it by the total caption count of a concept
280   in the dataset and use that as the concept’s frequency estimate. For concepts with less than 100K
281   candidate images, we simply use all the images that are positively classified. Note that several URLs
282
      in the LAION datasets are dead, a common phenomenon for URL based datasets (“link rot” Carlini
      et al. (2024); Lakic et al. (2023)). On average, we successfully retrieved 74% of the candidate images.
283
284
      5.2   C OMPUTING I MITATION S CORE
285
286   Challenges Computing the imitation score entails determining how similar a concept is in a generated
287   image compared to its source images from the training data. Several approaches were proposed to
288
      accomplish this task, such as FID and CLIPScore Salimans et al. (2016); Heusel et al. (2017); Sajjadi
      et al. (2018); Hessel et al. (2021); Podell et al. (2024). To measure similarity, these approaches
289
      compute the similarity between the distributions of the embeddings of the generated and training
290
      images of a concept. The embeddings are obtained using image embedders like Inception model in
291   case of FID and CLIP in case of CLIPScore. These image embedders often perform reasonably well
292   in measuring similarity between images of common objects which constitutes most of their training
293   data. However, they cannot reliably measure the similarity between two very similar concepts like the
294   faces of two individuals or art style of two artists (Somepalli et al., 2024; Hessel et al., 2021; Ahmadi
295   & Agrawal, 2024; Jayasumana et al., 2024). Therefore, MIMETIC2 uses domain specific image
296   embedders to measure similarity between two concepts from that domain. It uses a face embedding
297   model (Deng et al., 2022) for measuring face similarity and an art style embedding model Somepalli
298   et al. (2024) for measuring art style similarity. Even the specific choice of these models is crucial.
299
      For instance, in early experiments we used Facenet (Schroff et al., 2015), and observe it struggles to
      distinguish between individuals of certain demographics, causing drastic differences in the imitation
300
      scores between demographics. We provide more details on these early experiments in Appendix L,
301
      and show that our final choice of embedding models work well on different demographics.
302
303   Estimating Imitation Score To measure the imitation score we embed the generated images and
304   filtered training images of a concept (obtained in §5.1) using the same domain specific image embed-
305   der we used in §5.1, and report the imitation score as the average imitation between all generated and
306
      training images. We obtain the embeddings of the generated and training images for each concept,
      and measure imitation by computing the cosine similarity between them.
307
308   To ensure that the automatic measure of similarity correlates with human perception, we also
309
      conduct experiments with human subjects and measure the correlation between the similarities
      obtained automatically and in the human subject experiments. We find high correlation between the
310
      automatically obtained scores and human perception of similarity for both domains (§6).
311
312   5.3   D ETECTING THE Imitation Threshold
313
314   After computing the frequencies and the imitation scores for each concept, we sort them in an
315
      ascending order of their image frequencies. This generates a sequence of points, each of which
      is a pair of image frequency and the imitation score of a concept. We apply a standard change
316
      detection algorithm, PELT (Killick et al., 2012), to find the image frequency where the imitation
317
      score significantly changes. Change detection is a classic statistical approach for which the objective
318   is to find the points where the mean value of a sequence changes significantly. It is used for detecting
319   changes such as shifts in stock market trends or network traffic anomalies. Several algorithms were
320   proposed for change detection (van den Burg & Williams, 2022). We choose PELT because of
321   its linear time complexity in computing the change point. We choose the first change point as the
322   imitation threshold (see Appendix I for details about all change points). The application of change
323   detection assumes that increasing the image counts beyond a certain threshold leads to a large jump
      in the imitation scores, and we find this assumption to be accurate in our experimental results.


                                                         6
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

                                             SD1.5   371 ± 23      302 ± 113     302 ± 65    212 ± 29

                       LAION-5B              SD2.1   617 ± 148     385 ± 117     330 ± 142   292 ± 107
330
331
      To obtain error bounds for our results we perform a permutation test with sampling 300 concepts per
332
      domain and dataset, and repeat it 1,000 times. We report the mean and standard deviation results over
333
      the thresholds estimated by such experiments.
334
335   6     R ESULTS : T HE Imitation Threshold
336
      We apply MIMETIC2 to estimate the imitation threshold for each model-data pair, and present the
337
      results in Table 3. The imitation thresholds for SD1.1 on celebrities and politicians are 399 ± 85 and
338
      284 ± 87 respectively. And the imitation thresholds for classical and modern art styles are 304 ± 56
339
      and 208 ± 26 respectively. Interestingly, SD1.1 and SD1.5 have almost the same thresholds for all the
340
      four sets across both the domains. Notably, both SD1.1 and SD1.5 are trained on LAION2B-en. The
341   imitation thresholds for SD2.1, which is trained on the larger LAION-5B dataset are higher than the
342   thresholds for SD1.1 and SD1.5. The imitation threshold for SD2.1 on celebrities and politicians are
343   617 ± 148 and 385 ± 117 respectively, and on classical and modern art styles are 330 ± 142 and 292
344   ± 107 respectively. We hypothesize that the difference in performance of SD2.1 and SD1.5 is due to
345   the difference in their text encoders (O’Connor, 2022) (note: differences in performance between
346   SD1.5 and SD2.1 were also reported by several users on online forums). To test this hypothesis, we
347   compute the imitation thresholds for politicians for all SD models in series 1: SD1.1, SD1.2, SD1.3,
348   SD1.4, and SD1.5. We find that the imitation thresholds for all these models are almost the same. We
349   provide the imitation thresholds for all series 1 models in Appendix H.
350   Note that celebrities have a higher imitation threshold than politicians. We hypothesize this happens
351   due to inherent differences in the data distribution between these two sets, which makes it harder
352   to learn the concept of celebrities than politicians. To test this hypothesis, we compute the average
353   number of images that has one person for people with less than 1,000 images in the pretraining
354   dataset. We find that politicians have about twice the number of single person images compared to
355
      celebrities. As such, images that have a single person (the concept of interest) increase the ability of
      the model to learn from them, thus lowering the imitation threshold for politicians.
356
357
      We also present the plots of the imitation scores as a function of the image frequencies of the concepts
      in the four sets we experiment with. Figures 5a and 5b show the imitation graphs of celebrities and
358
      classical art styles, respectively for SD1.1. The x-axis is the frequency and the y-axis is the imitation
359
      score (averaged over the five image generation prompts) for each concept, sorted in increasing order
360   of frequency. Note that for this graph, we consider the situation where the assumption holds for all
361   the 400 concepts and hence the imitation threshold is computed using all the concepts. We showcase
362   the imitation graphs for all other models and sets in Appendix J, which follow similar trends.
363   In Figure 5a, we observe that the imitation scores for individuals with low image frequencies are
364   close to 0 (left side), and increase as the image frequencies increase (towards the right side). The
365   highest similarity is 0.5 and it is for individuals in the rightmost region of the plot. We observe a low
366   variance in the imitation scores across prompts, and also note that this variance does not depend on
367   the image frequencies (0.0003 ± 0.0005) – indicating that the performance of the face embedding
368   model does not depend on the popularity of the individual.
369   Similarly, in Figure 5b, we observe that imitation scores for art styles with low image frequencies are
370   low (close to 0.2 on the left side), and increase as the image frequencies increase (towards the right
371   side). The highest similarity is 0.76 and it is for the artists in the rightmost region of the plot. We
372   also observe a low variance across the generation prompts, and the variance does not depend on the
373   popularity of the artist (0.003 ± 0.003).
374   Human Perception Evaluation To determine if the automatic measure of similarity between the
375   generated and training images correlate with human perception, we conduct experiments with human
376   subjects. We asked the participants to rate generated images on the Likert scale Likert (1932) of 1-5
377   based on their similarity to real images of celebrities (the same ones used for measuring the imitation
      score). To avoid any bias, the participants were not informed of the research objective of this work.

432
433
434
435
436
437   (a) Outlier Kind 1. Thandiwe Newton is also aliased as Thandie New- (b) Outlier Kind 2. Most of the images whose captions mention Cacee
      ton. Since MIMETIC2 only collects images whose caption mentions Cobb have multiple people in them, only 6 images have her as the only
438
      Thandiwe Newton, this leads to underestimation of image counts.     person, leading to a low imitation score in generated images.
439
      Figure 6: Examples of the two kinds of outliers. The top and bottom rows show the real and SD1.1 generated
440
      images respectively. Images were generated using the prompt: “a photorealistic close-up image of name.”
441
442
      We also note that the range of the imitation scores of different domains have different y-axis scales.
443   This is due to the difference in embedding models used in both cases. The face embedding model can
444   distinguish between two faces much better than the art style model can distinguish between two styles
445   (see Appendices F and G), and therefore the scores for the concepts on the left side of the imitation
446   threshold is around 0 for face imitation and 0.2 for style imitation. The face embedding model also
447   gives lower score to the faces of the same person, compared to the style embedding model’s score for
448   images of the same art styles, and therefore the highest scores for face imitation is 0.5, whereas it is
449   0.76 for art style imitation. However, the absolute values on the y-axis do not matter for estimating
450   the imitation threshold as long as the trend is similar, which is the case for both domains.
451   Evaluation of the Imitation Thresholds Evaluating the accuracy of the imitation thresholds
452   MIMETIC2 finds is a very expensive task, it requires conducting the optimal experiment described in
453   Section 3 (training O(log m) models with different number of images of each concept). Therefore we
454   choose an approximate method to test MIMETIC2 ’s accuracy. Concretely, we calibrate MIMETIC2
455   using one of the sets in each domain (Celebrities for Human Faces           and Classical art styles for
456   Art Style ) and test the calibrated approach on the other set (Politicians for Human Faces          and
457
      Modern art styles for Art Style ). In essence, the sets that we use to calibrate MIMETIC2 on, acts
      as the “train datasets” and the other sets acts as the “test dataset”. Note that these sets are mutually
458
      exclusive. Table 3 shows that the imitation thresholds MIMETIC2 finds for “train” and “test” sets in
459
      each domain are very close (the difference across the sets for both human faces and art styles is less
460   than 5e-10% of the pretraining dataset size), indicating that the imitation thresholds MIMETIC2
461   finds are accurate. Note that this is another evidence for MIMETIC2 ’s accuracy in addition to the
462   evidence already provided by the human subject experiments.
463
464   7     A NALYSIS : I NVESTIGATING O UTLIERS
465
466
      The imitation score plots in the previous section, while showcasing a clear trend, have several outliers.
      In this section, we analyze such outliers and present examples in Figure 6 (additional outliers can be
467
      found in Figures 29 and 30 in the Appendix).
468
469   Low Image Counts and High Imitation Scores Figure 6a shows an example of such a case:
470   Thandiwe Newton’s image count is 172 in LAION2B-en, lower than the imitation threshold for
471   celebrities: 364. However, her imitation score of 0.26 is much higher than those of neighboring
472   celebrities with similar image counts (with scores of 0.01 and 0.04). Further investigation reveals that
473
      Thandiwe Newton is also known as Thandie Newton. Since this alias may also be used to describe
      her in captions, MIMETIC2 may have underestimated her image counts. We repeat the process
474
      for estimating the image counts with the new alias, and find that Thandie Newton appears in 12,177
475
      images, bringing the cumulative image count to 12,349, which significantly surpasses the established
476   imitation threshold. The two aliases, whose total image count is considerably higher than the imitation
477   threshold, differ by only a single letter and are similarly represented by the model’s encoder (cosine
478   similarity of 0.96), which explains the high imitation score. We find that most of the celebrities from
479   the first kind of outliers are also known by other names which lead to underestimating their image
480   counts. For example, Belle Delphine (394 images) also goes by Mary Belle (310 images, for a total
481   of 704, and DJ Kool Herc (492 images) also goes by Kool Herc (269 images, for a total of 761).
482   The aliases explanation also largely explains the outliers in art style imitation. For instance, artist
483   Gustav Adolf Mossa (19 images) also goes by just Mossa (15850 images), artist Nicolas Toussaint
484   Charlet (78 images) also goes by just Nicolas Toussaint (533 images), and artist Wilhelm Von
485   Kaulbach (81 images) also goes by Von Kaulbach (978 images). See Figures 31 and 32 in the
      appendix for the real and generated images of these artists.


                                                                        9
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


486
      High Image Counts and Low Imitation Scores Several celebrities have higher image counts than
487   the imitation threshold, but low imitation scores. Unlike the previous case, we were unable to find
488   a common cause that explains all these outliers. However, we find explanations for specific cases.
489   For example, a staggering proportion of the training images for such celebrities have multiple people
490   in them. For example, out of the 706 total images of Cacee Cobb, only 6 images have her as the
491   only person in the image (see Figure 6b). Similarly, out of 1,296 total images of Sofia Hellqvist, only
492   67 images have her as the only person and out of the 472 total images of Charli D’ Amelio, only
493   82 images have her as the only person. We hypothesize that having multiple concepts in an image
494   impedes the proper mapping of the concept’s text embedding to its image embedding, which can
495
      explain the low imitation score for these concepts. We leave it to future work to further study the
      connection between the number of concepts in an image and model’s ability to imitate these concepts.
496
497
      8    D ISCUSSION AND L IMITATIONS
498
499   Equal Effect Assumption An assumption in the formulation of MIMETIC2 is that every image of a
500   concept contributes equally to the learning of the concept. Although this is a standard assumption
501   in sample complexity works (Valiant, 1984; Udandarao et al., 2024; Wen et al., 2024; Wang et al.,
502
      2017), not all images are created equal. While analyzing celebrities’ images for instance, we often
      find that individuals whose images are mostly close-ups of a single person have a higher imitation
503
      score than individuals whose images are cluttered by multiple people, since concept-centered images
504
      enhance their learnability. We hope to investigate this assumption in future work.
505
506   Factors Affecting the Imitation Threshold In this work we attribute the imitation of a concept to its
507   image count. However, image count – although a crucial factor – is not the only factor that affects
508
      imitation. Several other factors like image resolution, image diversity, alignment between images and
      their captions, the variance between images of a concept may affect imitation.
509
510   Several training time factors like the optimization objective, learning schedule, training data order,
511   model capacity, model architecture also affect the imitation threshold. We discuss the difference
512
      in the imitation thresholds of SD1.1, SD1.5 and SD2.1 is attributed to the difference in their text
      encoders. SD1.1 and SD1.5 use CLIP model Radford et al. (2021) as their text encoder and SD2.1
513
      uses OpenCLIP Ilharco et al. (2021) as its text encoder. Note that while these may impact the behavior
514
      of the model, our work is interested in a particular model-data pair, for which we investigate. While
515   we conduct experiments for most models whose training data is available, we do not claim that our
516   results would generalize to other models or datasets, and hope that in future more such models and
517   datasets are open-sourced, so that we can test the generalizability of our imitation thresholds. Note
518   that, MIMETIC2 finds the imitation threshold for the pretraining regime. We hope to investigate the
519   thresholds when a model is finetuned on one or a set of concepts in future work.
520
      Considerations for Legal Conclusions Our work estimates the imitation thresholds under certain
521
      assumptions. While we hope our work could be used for copyright and privacy claims, our results
522
      should be put in content of the assumptions we make (§3) In addition, we do not claim our results
523
      generalize to all model/data pairs, and more work needs to be done on this topic.
524
525
526
      9    C ONCLUSIONS
527   Text-to-image models can imitate their training images (Somepalli et al., 2023a; Carlini et al., 2023a;
528   Somepalli et al., 2023b). This behavior is potentially concerning because these models’ training
529   datasets often include copyrighted and licensed images. Imitating such images would be grounds
530   for violation of copyright and privacy laws. In this work, we seek to find the number of instances
531
      of a concept that a text-to-image model needs in order to imitate it – the imitation threshold. We
      posit this as a new problem, Finding the Imitation Threshold (FIT) and propose an efficient method,
532
      MIMETIC2 , for finding such thresholds. Our method utilizes pretrained models to estimate this
533
      threshold for human faces and art styles imitation using three text-to-image models trained on two
534   different pretraining datasets. We find the imitation threshold of these models to be in the range
535   of 200-600 images depending on the setup. As such, on the domains we evaluate in this work, our
536   results indicate that models cannot imitate concepts that appear less than 200 times in the training
537   data. By estimating the imitation threshold, we provide insights on successful concept imitation based
538   on their training frequencies. Our results have striking implications on both the text-to-image models
539   users and developers. These thresholds can inform text-to-image model developers what concepts are
      in risk of being imitated, and on the other hand, serve as a basis for copyright and privacy complaints.


                                                        10
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


540
      10    E THICS S TATEMENT
541
542   This work is targeted towards understanding and mitigating the negative social impacts of widely
543   available text-to-image models. To minimize the impact of our study on potential copyright infringe-
544   ment and privacy violations, we used existing text-to-image models and generation techniques. To
545   strike a balance between reproducibility and ethics, we release the code used in this work, but not the
546   real and synthetic images used (we also provide detailed documentation of our data curation process
547
      in the code to ensure transparency).
548   Human Subject Experiments For the human subject experiments performed in this work, we
549   followed the university guideline. The study was IRB approved by the university and each participant
550   was made aware of the goals and risks of the study. In accordance with local minimum wage
551   requirements, we compensated each participant with Amazon gift cards of $25/hour for their time.

828   B     A DDITIONAL R ELATED W ORK
829
830
      Imitation in Text-to-Image Models: Somepalli et al. (2023a); Carlini et al. (2023a) demonstrated
      that diffusion models can memorize and imitate duplicate images from their training data (they
831
      use ‘replication’ to refer to this phenomenon). Casper et al. (2023) corroborated the evidence by
832
      showing that these models imitated art styles of 70 artists with high accuracy (as classified by a CLIP
833   model) when prompted to generated images in their styles (a group of artists also sued Stability AI
834   claiming that their widely-used text-to-image models imitated their art style, violating copyright laws
835   Saveri & Butterick (2023)). However, these works did not study how much repetition of a concept’s
836   images would lead the model to imitate them. Studying this relation is important as it serves to guide
837   institutions training these models who want to comply with copyright and privacy laws.
838
839   Mitigation of Imitation in Text-to-Image Models:             Several works proposed to mitigate the
840
      negative impacts of text-to-image models. Shan et al. (2023) proposed GLAZE that adds imperceptible
      noise to the art works such that diffusion models are unable to imitate artist styles. A similar approach
841
      was proposed to hinder learning human faces (Shan et al., 2020). Wang et al. (2024) proposed adding
842
      noise to training images, which can be used to detect if a model has been trained on those images. Lu
843   et al. (2024) propose pushing the generated images away from the distribution of training images to
844   minimize mitigation. Kumari et al. (2023); Gandikota et al. (2024) proposed algorithms to remove
845   specific styles, explicit content, and other copyrighted material learned by text-to-image models.
846   On a related note, Xie et al. (2024) proposed Diffusion-ReTrac that finds training images that most
847   influenced a generated image, and thereby provide a fair attribution to training data contributors.
848
849   C     VALIDITY OF THE A SSUMPTIONS
850
851   Table 5: Average difference in the imitation scores for concepts whose image counts differ by less than 10. The
852   difference in the imitation scores are close to 0, empirically validating the distribution invariance assumption.
853                     Domain                  Dataset             Avg. difference in imitation score
854
855
                  Human Faces                 Celebrities                            0.0007
856
                  Human Faces                 Politicians                            0.0023
                   Art Style              Classical Art Style                       -0.0088
857
                   Art Style              Modern Art Style                          -0.0013
858
859   In this section, we empirically test the statistical validity of the assumptions we make in Section 3.
860   The first assumption states that there is a distributional invariance across concepts. If this is true, then
861   the imitation scores of two concepts (from the same domain) whose image counts are similar, should
862   also be similar to each other. To test whether this is empirically true for the domains we experiment
863   with, we measure the difference in the imitation scores for concepts whose image counts differ by
      less than 10 images and report the difference averaged over all such pairs. Table 5 shows that the


                                                             16
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


864
      average difference in the imitation scores for all the pairs, for all the datasets we experiment with, is
865   very close to 0. This provides empirical validation of the distribution invariance assumption.
866
867
868
      D     C APTION O CCURRENCE A SSUMPTION
869
      For estimating the concept’s counts in the pretraining dataset we make a simplifying assumption: a
870
      concept can be present in the image only if it is mentioned in a paired caption. While this assumption
871   isn’t true in general, we show that for the domains we experiment with, it mostly holds in practice.
872
873   Table 6: Face count of the ten most popular celebrities in 100K random LAION images. The small percentage
      of the images we miss shows that our assumption of counting the images where a concept is mentioned in the
874
      caption is empirically reasonable.
875
876                            Face Count in     Face Count in Images       Percentage of         Number of
       Celebrity
877                             100K images      with Caption Mention      Missed Images       Missed Images
878    Floyd Mayweather               1                     0                  0.001%               23K
879    Oprah Winfrey                  2                     0                  0.002%               46K
880    Ronald Reagan                  6                     3                  0.003%               69K
881    Ben Affleck                    0                     0                   0.0%                  0
882    Anne Hathaway                  0                     0                   0.0%                  0
883    Stephen King                   0                     0                   0.0%                  0
884
       Johnny Depp                    9                     1                  0.008%              184K
       Abraham Lincoln               52                     1                  0.051%              1.17M
885
       Kate Middleton                34                     1                  0.033%              759K
886
       Donald Trump                  16                     0                  0.016%              368K
887
888
      For this purpose, we download 100K random images from LAION2B-en, and run the face detection
889   (used in Section 5) on all images, and count the faces of the ten most popular celebrities in our
890   sampled set of celebrities. Out of the 100K random images, about 57K contain faces. For each
891   celebrity, we compute the similarity between all the faces in the downloaded images and the faces in
892   the reference images of these celebrities. If the similarity is above the threshold of 0.46, we consider
893   that face to belong to the celebrity (this threshold is determined in Appendix F to distinguish if two
894   images are of the same person or not). Table 6 shows the number of faces we found for each celebrity
895   in the 100K random LAION images. We also show the face counts among these images whose
896   captions mention the celebrity. We find that 1) the highest frequency an individual appears in an
897
      image without their name mentioned in the caption is 51 (Abraham Lincoln is mentioned once in the
      caption and he appears a total of 52 times), and 2) the highest percentage of image frequency that we
898
      miss is 0.051%, and 3) most of the other miss rates are much smaller (close to 0). Such low miss
899
      rates demonstrate that our assumption of counting images when a concept is mentioned in the caption
900   is empirically reasonable.
901
      We also note that this assumption would fail if we were computing image frequencies for concepts
902
      that are so widely common that one would not even mention them in a caption, for example, phone,
903
      shoes, or trees.
904
905
906
      E     C OLLECTION OF R EFERENCE I MAGES
907
      E.1   C OLLECTION OF R EFERENCE I MAGES FOR H UMAN FACES D OMAIN
908
909   The goal of collecting reference images is to use them to filter the images of the pretraining dataset.
910   These images are treated as the gold standard reference images of a person and images collected from
911   pretraining dataset are compared to these images. If the similarity is higher than a threshold then that
912   image is considered to belong to that person (see Section 5 for details). We describe an automatic
913
      manner of collecting the reference images. The high level idea is to collect the images from Google
      Search and automatically select a subset of those images that are of the same concept (same person’s
914
      face or same artist’s art). Since this is a crucial part of the overall algorithm, we manually vet the
915
      reference images for all the concepts to ensure that they all contain the same concept.
916
917   Collection of Reference Images for Human Face Imitation: We collect reference images for
      celebrities and politicians using a three step process (also shown in Algorithm 1):


                                                         17
          Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


918
          1. Candidate set: First, we retrieved the first hundred images by searching a person’s name on
919          Google Images. We used SerpAPI SerpApi (2024) as a wrapper perform the searches.
920
921
          2. Selecting from the candidate set: Images retrieved from the internet are noisy and might not
             contain the person we are looking for. Therefore we filter images that contain the person from
922
             the candidate set of images. For this purpose, we use a face recognition model. We embed all the
923
             faces in the retrieved images using a face embedding model and measure the cosine similarity
924          between each one of them. The goal is to search for a set of faces that belong to the same person
925          and therefore will have a high cosine similarity to each other.
926
             One strategy is for the faces to form a graph where the vertices are the face embeddings and the
927          edges connecting two embeddings have a weight equal to the cosine similarity between them, and
928          we select a dense k-subgraph (Lanciano et al., 2024) from this graph. Selecting such a subgraph
929          means finding a mutually homogeneous subset. We can find the vertices of this dense k-subgraph
930          by cardinality-constrained submodular function minimization (Bilmes, 2022; Nagano et al., 2011)
931          on a facility location function (Bilmes, 2022). We run this minimization and select a subset of
932          images (at least of size ten) that has the highest average cosine similarity between each pair of
933          images.
934       3. Manual verification: Selecting the faces with the highest average similarity is not enough. This
935          is because in many cases the largest set of faces in the candidate set are not of the person we look
936          for, but for someone closely associated with them, in which case, the selected images are of the
937          other person. For example, all the selected faces for Miguel Bezos were actually of Jeff Bezos.
938          Therefore, we manually verify all the selected faces for each person. In the situation where the
939          selected faces are wrong, we manually collect the images for them, for example, for Miguel Bezos.
940
             We collect at least 5 reference images for all celebrities.
941
942       Algorithm 1: Collection of Reference Images for Human Face Imitation
943
        Input: Person’s name P
944
945
        Output: Verified Set of Images of P
      1 images ← SerpAPI(P) ;                    ▷ Retrieve initial image set using SerpAPI
946
947   2 candidateSet ← Submodular_Minimization(images) ;        ▷ Select candidate set using
948        submodular minimization
949   3   verif iedSet ← manualVerification(candidateSet) ; ▷ Manually verify the candidate set
950
951
952
          Collection of Reference Images for Art Styles We collect reference images for each artist (each
          artist is assumed to have a distinct art style) from Wikiart, the online encyclopedia for art works.
953
          Since the art works of each artist were meticulously collected and vetted by the artist community, we
954
          consider all the images collected from Wikiart as the reference art images for that artist.
955
956
957
          F     I MPLEMENTATION D ETAILS OF MIMETIC2 FOR H UMAN FACE I MITATION
958
          F.1   F ILTERING OF T RAINING I MAGES
959
960       Images whose captions mention the concept of interest often do not contain it (as shown with Mary-
961       Lee Pfeiffer in Figure 4). As such, we filter images where the concept does not appear in the image,
962       which we detect using a dedicated classifier. In what follows we describe the filtering mechanism.
963       Collecting Reference Images:
964
          We collect reference images for each person using SerpAPI as described in Appendix E. These images
965
          are the gold standard images that we manually vet to ensure that they contain the target person of
966       interest (see Appendix E for the details). We use the reference images to filter out the images in the
967       pretraining dataset that are not of this person. Concretely, for each person we use a face embedding
968       model Deng et al. (2020) to measure the similarity between the faces in the reference images and the
969       faces in the images from the pretraining datasets whose captions mention this person. If the similarity
970       of a face in the pretraining images to any of the faces in the reference images is above a certain
971       threshold, that face is considered to belong to the person of interest. We determine this threshold to
          distinguish faces of the same person from faces of different persons in the next paragraph. Note that


                                                            18
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


972
973
974
975
976
977                  (a) Training images of Samuel L. Jackson that show significant variations in his face (age, hair, and beard).

978
979
980
981
982               (b) Generated images of Samuel L. Jackson that show the model has captured a specific characteristic of his face
983               (middle-aged, bald, with no or little beard).
984                                 Figure 8: Real and generated images of Samuel L. Jackson.
985
986
987    this procedure already filter outs any image that does not contain a face, because the face embedding
988
       model would only embed an image if it detects a face in that image.
989    Determining Filtering Threshold: The next step is
990    to determine the threshold for which we consider two                                Similarity between same people

                                                                                           Similarity between different people

999    well separated, with the lowest similarity value between Figure 7: Average cosine similarity between
1000   the faces of the same person being 0.56 and the highest the faces of the same people (blue colored)
1001   similarity value between the faces of different persons and of the faces of different people (red col-
1002   being 0.36. Therefore any threshold value between 0.36 ored), measured across the reference images
1003   and 0.56 can separate two face of the same person, from of the celebrities.
1004   the faces of different people. In our experiments, we use
1005   the midpoint threshold of 0.46 (true positive rate (tpr) of
1006   100%; false positive rate (fpr) of 0%) to filter any face in the pretraining images that do not belong
1007
       to the person of interest. The filtering process gives us both the image frequency a person in the
       pretraining data, and the pretraining images that we compare the faces in the generated images to
1008
       measure the imitation score.
1009
1010
1011
1012   F.2    M EASUREMENT OF I MITATION S CORE
1013
1014   To measure the imitation between the training and generated images of a person, we compute the
1015   cosine similarity between the face embeddings of the faces in their generated images and their filtered
1016   training images from the previous step. However, measuring the similarity using all the pretraining
1017
       images can underestimate the actual imitation. This is because several individuals have significant
       variations in their faces in the pretraining images and the text-to-image model does not capture all
1018
       these variations. For example, consider the pretraining images of Samuel L. Jackson in Figure 8a.
1019
       These images have significant variations in beard, hair, and age. However, when the text-to-image
1020   model is prompted to generate images of Samuel L. Jackson, the generated images in Figure 8b
1021   only show a specific facial characteristic of him (middle-aged, bald, with no or little beard). Since
1022   MIMETIC2 ’s goal is not to measure if a text-to-image model captures all the variations of a person,
1023   we want to reward the model even if it has only captured a particular characteristic (which it has in
1024   this case of Samuel L. Jackson). Therefore, instead of comparing the similarity of generated images
1025   to all the training images, we compare the similarity to only the ten training images that have the
       highest cosine similarity to the generated images on average.


                                                                         19
        Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1026
        G                          I MPLEMENTATION D ETAILS OF MIMETIC2 FOR A RT S TYLE I MITATION
1027
1028    G.1                             F ILTERING OF T RAINING I MAGES
1029
1030
       For art style imitation, we consider each artist to have a unique style. We collect the images from the
       pretraining dataset whose captions mention the name of the artist whose art style imitation we want to
1031
       measure. Similar to the case of human face imitation, we want to filter out the pretraining images of
1032
       an artist that in reality was not created by that artist, but their captions mention them. We implement
1033   the filtering process in two stages. In the first stage, we filter out non-art images in the pretraining
1034   dataset (note that the captions of these images still mention the artist, but the images themselves are
1035   not art works) and in the second stage we filter out art works of other artists (the captions of these
1036   images mention the artist of interest and the image itself is also an art work, but by a different artist).
1037   The implementation details for each stage is as follows:
1038
                                         Art images                                                                     5000          Art images
1039                                     Non-art images      Threshold: 0.182                                                         Non-art images     Threshold: 0.177
                                         Threshold                                                                                    Threshold
1040                      4000
                                                                                                                        4000

       Number of images

                                                                                                                        3000

                                                                                                                        2000

1045
1046                      1000                                                                                          1000

1047
1048                        0
                                 0.00       0.05      0.10     0.15     0.20      0.25   0.30
                                                                                                                          0
                                                                                                                               0.00      0.05     0.10      0.15   0.20     0.25     0.30   0.35
                                                    Cosine similarity to 'an artwork'                                                            Cosine similarity to 'an artwork'
1049
1050    (a) Histogram of the cosine similarity of embeddings of art and non-                          (b) Histogram of the cosine similarity of embeddings of art and non-
        art images to embeddings of ‘an artwork’ for classical artists.                               art images to embeddings of ‘an artwork’ for modern artists.
1051
        Figure 9: The first filtering step involves determining the threshold to distinguish between art and non-art images
1052
        from the pretraining images, for which we compare the similarity of the image’s embedding to the embedding of
1053    the text “an artwork”.
1054
1055   Filtering Non-Art Images: To filter non-art images from the pretraining dataset, we use a classifier
1056   that separates art images from non-art images. Concretely, we embed the pretraining images using a
1057   CLIP ViT-H/14 Ilharco et al. (2021) image encoder and measure the cosine similarity of the image
1058   embeddings and the text embeddings of the string ‘an artwork’, embedded using the text encoder
1059   of the same model. Only when the similarity between the embeddings is higher than a threshold
1060   described below, we consider those pretraining images as an artwork. To determine this threshold,
1061
       we choose a similarity score that separates art images from non-art images. We use the images from
       the Wikiarts dataset Saleh & Elgammal (2016) as the (positive) art images and MS COCO dataset
1062
       images Lin et al. (2014) as the (negative) non-art images. Note that MS COCO dataset was collected
1063
       by photographing everyday objects that art was not part of, making it a valid set of negative examples
1064   of art.
1065
       We plot the histogram of cosine similarity of the embeddings of art and non-art images to the text
1066
       embedding of ‘an artwork’ (see Figures 9a and 9b. We observe that the art and non-art images both
1067   the artist groups are well separated (although not perfect, Figure 10 and Figure 11 shows examples
1068   of misclassified and correctly classfied images from both datasets). We choose the threshold that
1069   maximizes the F1 score of the separation (0.182 for the classical artists and 0.177 for the modern
1070   artists).
1071   Filtering Images of Other Art Styles: Similar to the case of human faces, not all art images whose
1072   captions mention an artist were created by that artist. We want to filter out such images. For this
1073   purpose, we collect reference images for each artist (see Appendix E for details) and use them to
1074   classify the training images that belong to the artist of interest. Concretely, we measure the similarity
1075   between the pretraining images and the reference images of each artist, and only retain images whose
1076   similarity to the reference images is higher than a threshold.
1077   To determine this threshold, we measure the similarity between pairs of art images of the same artists
1078   and pairs of art images from different artists. We embed the images using an art style embedding
1079   model Somepalli et al. (2024) and plot the histogram of similarities between art images of the same
       artist (blue colored) and art images of different artists (red colored) in Figure 12a for classical artists

       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

             in them and therefore are classified in that category. These images were selected in MS COCO for different categories like scissors,
1087         chair, parking meter, and vase.

1108   and Figure 12b for modern artists. We see that the two histograms are well separated (although
1109   not perfect, Figure 13 shows paintings by two artists whose art style is very similar and cannot
1110   be distinguished by our threshold). We choose the threshold that maximizes the F1 score of the
1111
       separation between these two groups (0.278 for classical artists and 0.288 for modern artists). The
       retained images give us both the image counts of each artist and the training images that we compare
1112
       to the generated images to measure the imitation score.
1113
1114
1115   G.2     S IMILARITY M EASUREMENT
1116
1117   We embed all the generated images and the filtered pretraining images using the art style embedding
1118   model (Somepalli et al., 2024) and measure the cosine similarity between each pair of generated and
1119
       pretraining images. Similar to the case of the human faces, we do not want to underestimate the art
       style similarity between the generated and training images by comparing the generated images to all
1120
       the training images of this artist. Therefore, we measure the similarity of generated images to the ten
1121
       training images that are on average the most similar to the generated images.
1122
1123
1124   H     I MITATION T HRESHOLDS OF SD MODELS IN S ERIES 1 AND 2
1125
1126
       Our experimental results in Section 6 found that for most domains the imitation thresholds for SD1.1
1127
       and SD1.5 are almost the same, while being higher for SD2.1. We hypothesized that the difference is
1128   due to their different text encoders. All models in SD1 series use the same text encoder from CLIP,
1129   whereas SD2.1 uses the text encoder from OpenCLIP. To test the validity of this hypothesis, we
1130   repeated the experiments for all models in SD1 series for politicians and computed their imitation
1131   thresholds. Table 7 shows the thresholds for the politicians. We find that the imitation thresholds for
1132   all the models in SD1 series is almost the same, and is lower than the threshold for SD2.1 model.
1133   This evidence supports our hypothesis of the difference in the text-encoders being the main reason
       for the difference in the imitation thresholds.

     Ideally, a face embedding model should have low FMR and high TMR. An important variant of these
     FMR measures how many times does a model says two people are the same when they are not and
     justify the choice of the final choice of our face embedding model. Face embedding models are

1501                                                                                                                                       Face Recognition Models
1502     Figure 33: False-match rate (FMR) of all the face embedding models across the six demographic groups.
1503     Amazon Rekognition and InsightFace have the lowest FMR values. Moreover, these two models have lowest
1504     disparity of FMR over the demographic groups.
1505
1506     metrics is the disparity of FMR and TMR of a model across different demographic groups. Ideally, a
1507     model should have low disparity in these metrics across different demographics. We also focus on
1508     the variance of these metrics across demographics in making the final choice.
1509     We evaluate the FMR and TMR of eight different face embedding models (seven open-sourced and
1510     one proprietary). The open-source models were chosen based on their popularity on Github (Serengil
1511     & Ozpinar, 2020; Deng et al., 2022; 2020), and we also experiment with Amazon Rekognition, a
         proprietary model. For evaluating the disparity of these metrics across different demographic groups

                               Amazon Rekognition                   Insightface                       GhostFaceNet_W1 GhostFaceNet_V1                                                       ArcFace                         Facenet                      Facenet512                         DeepFace
1518                                                                                                                                               Face Recognition Models
1519      Figure 34: True-match rate (TMR) of all the face embedding models across the six demographic groups. Amazon
1520      Rekognition model has the highest TMR values.
1521
         we grouped celebrities in six demographic groups primarily categorized according to skin color tone
1522
         (black, brown, and white) and perceived gender (male and female; for simplicity). Each of the six
1523
         groups had 10 celebrities (a total of 60), with no intersection between them. The categorization was
1524     done manually by looking at the reference images of the celebrities. For each celebrity, we collect 10
1525     reference images from the internet by using the procedure described in Appendix E. We use these
1526     images to compare the FMR and TMR of the face recognition models, as these images are the gold
1527     standard images of a person.
1528     FMR Computation: We compute the mean cosine similarity between the face embeddings of
1529     one individual and the faces of all other individuals in that group, and repeat the procedure for all
1530     individuals in a demographic group.
1531     TMR Computation: We compute the mean cosine similarity between the embeddings of all the
1532     faces of an individuals and repeat the procedure for all the individuals in a demographic group.
1533     Figure 33 and Figure 34 shows the FMR and TMR for six demographic groups for all the face
1534     embedding models. All the open-sourced models, except InsightFace, either have a high disparity
1535     in FMR values across the demographic groups (ArcFace, Facenet, Facenet512, DeepFace) or have
1536     very low TMR (GhostFaceNet_W1, GhostFaceNet_V1). We choose InsightFace for our experiments
1537     because of it has 1) a low overall FMR, 2) decent TMR, 3) a low disparity of FMR and TMR
1538     across the demographic groups, and 4) is open-sourced. Having a low disparity of the metrics across
1539     individuals of different demographic groups is crucial for an accurate estimation of the imitation
1540
         threshold. The Amazon Rekognition model would also be a viable choice based on these metrics,
         however, it is not open-sourced and therefore expensive for our experiments.

1697
1698   N      C OMPUTE U SED
1699
1700   Estimated Computational Cost of the Optimal Experiment The computational cost to train the
1701   popular text-to-image model Stable Diffusion was $600K (Bastian, 2022). For the optimal experiment,
1702
       we would need to train O(log m) models, where m is the total number of available images of a
       concept Z j . So if a concept has 100,000 images, then we need to train log2 (100, 000) = 16 models
1703
       to optimally estimate the imitation threshold, which would cost about $10M.
1704
1705   MIMETIC2 ’s Computational Cost We use 8 L40 GPUs to generate images for the all text-to-
1706   image models in our work. Overall, we use them for 16 hours per prompt, per dataset, per model to
1707   generate images. We downloaded the images on the same machine using 40 CPU cores, a process that
1708   took about 8 hours per dataset. For generating the image embeddings, we use the same 8 L40 GPUs,
1709   a process that took about 16 hours per dataset. The computation of imitation score and plotting are
1710   done on single CPU core on the same machine, a process that takes less than 30 minutes per dataset.
